claims.text: ChangXin Memory Technologies — listing as 长鑫科技, ticker 688825 — opens subscription for its RMB 29.5 billion STAR Market IPO on July 16, 2026, the largest A-share listing of the year and the second-largest in the exchange's history[^2,61].
As of 2026-07-14 there is **no final price**. The preliminary inquiry ran July 13; the pricing announcement publishes July 15 [^3].

The next rung is arithmetic: ¥29.5 billion in proceeds divided by 6.688 billion new shares yields a ~¥4.41 floor, and 66.88 billion post-issue shares imply a ~¥295 billion minimum cap — a number the filing fixes but STAR rules let the bookbuild exceed, with the answer landing July 15 [^92,3].

The Shanghai Stock Exchange accepted the prospectus on 2025-12-30 — the first filing processed under the STAR Market's pre-review pilot — and the listing committee approved it unanimously on 2026-05-27, with registration submitted the same day [^5]. The CSRC granted registration permit 证监许可〔2026〕1344号 on 2026-06-12 [^6].

FY2025 revenue reached ¥61.799 billion, up 155.6%, with main-business gross margin swinging from 5.00% to 41.02% in a single year [^7]. Revenue over the same stretch was ¥8.287 billion, ¥9.087 billion, and ¥24.178 billion [^57].

CXMT booked attributable net losses of ¥8.328 billion in 2022, ¥16.340 billion in 2023, and ¥7.145 billion in 2024 — roughly ¥32 billion over three years, with an accumulated deficit of ¥36.65 billion still on the balance sheet at end-2025 [^58].

Revenue of ¥50.8 billion — {accent}82% of all of FY2025 in thirteen weeks{/} — with attributable net income of ¥24.76 billion, up 1,688.3% year over year [^9]. The H1 2026 forecast extends the run: ¥110–120 billion of revenue, ¥66–75 billion of consolidated net income, and ¥50–57 billion attributable, implying attributable growth of +2,244% to +2,544% [^9,86].

CXMT holds only **30.68%** of the Xinqiao fab entity (长鑫新桥) and **31.72%** of the Beijing fab entity (长鑫集电), consolidating them via acting-in-concert agreements controlling roughly 73–75% of votes — while Big Fund II directly holds 26.99% and 24.67% of those entities [^89].

One Omdia-derived series has CXMT at 3.97% of revenue in 2Q25 rising to {accent}7.67% by 4Q25{/} [^96]; a second Omdia-derived cut, published by Seoul Economic Daily on July 11, puts 4Q25 at 4.7% and Q1 2026 at 7.6% — a quarter-on-quarter near-doubling [^76].
SemiAnalysis's more conservative model has ~265k installed at end-2025, growing to ~350k by end-2026, within sight of Micron's ~385k [^22,11]. On wafers, CXMT is nearly the world's #3.

The prospectus confirms there is no slack left in the installed base: utilization climbed from 87.06% (2023) to 92.46% (2024) to {accent}95.73% in 2025{/} [^28], so every increment of 2026 output must come from newly installed tools, not sweating existing ones.

A DDR4 8Gb (1Gx8 3200) chip that traded around ==$1.75== on the TrendForce spot market in mid-May 2025 averaged {accent}$38.50{/} in the July 13, 2026 session — a roughly **22x** move in fourteen months, and still rising (+1.85% day-over-day as of 2026-07-13) [^32,36].

By 1Q26 conventional DRAM contract prices were up a record 90–95% QoQ; a 64GB DDR5 RDIMM that cost roughly $450 in 4Q25 crossed $900 in 1Q26. The pace is now decelerating — +58–63% in 2Q26, a forecast +13–18% for server DRAM in 3Q26 — partly because long-term-agreement price ceilings cap how fast contracts can reprice, not because supply has loosened [^34].
TrendForce puts HBM's share of top-3 DRAM wafer input at ~18% at end-2025, rising to ~22% by end-2026 and ~30% by end-2027 — while HBM's share of *bits* runs only 8%, 9%, and 13% over the same span.

CXMT's DDR5 is real, shipping, and physically verifiable — and the same teardown that proves it exists also prices the tax every wafer pays: a node the incumbents ran five years ago, a die roughly 40% larger than Samsung's equivalent, and a cost-per-bit more than 30% above the Big 3, while the one number that would show the tax shrinking — yield — is exactly the one nobody independent can confirm.

LPDDR5X at 8533 and 9600 Mbps entered mass production in May 2025, with 10667 Mbps in customer trials by October — an inline speed ladder of {sparkline:8533,9600,10667} Mbps — though spec parity is not node or cost parity: Samsung has run 12nm-package LPDDR5X since August 2024 [^24]. That mobile part now sits in over 30% of Chinese Android-brand phones ex-Huawei, with LPDDR above 40% of shipments [^25]

HBM2 is described as "small-scale production," HBM3 as "sample delivery," and ~98.3% of revenue comes from LPDDR (66.43%) and DDR (31.87%) — yet the use-of-proceeds table contains no HBM capacity-expansion line at all [^11]. End-2025 HBM capacity was ~5k wspm — under 2% of CXMT's ~265k wspm total [^11]
CXMT's plausible ~2M stacks in 2026 covers roughly 250-300k Ascend 910C packages, while Huawei stockpiled ~13M foreign stacks — 11.4M of them from Samsung — before the export window closed [^16].

An interagency committee approved Entity-Listing CXMT and 100+ other firms; Commerce is withholding publication [^41]. CXMT is **not** on the Commerce Entity List — the binding one that cuts off US-origin exports [^41].

Apple has begun technical validation of CXMT DRAM — the step preceding supplier approval — for China-market devices only, with no commercial commitment; CXMT parts are priced 10–30% below incumbents [^66].

The marquee proof point is Tencent: a long-term server-DRAM supply agreement worth more than RMB 20 billion (~$2.94 billion) covering several years, per Reuters [^63]. What is corroborated separately: CXMT is taking DDR5 server orders from both Tencent and ByteDance after completing customer validation [^64]

The sharper cut is the end-market one: server DRAM went from 8.39% of revenue in 2024 to 26.51% in 2025 [^11]. Top-five customers took 74.12%, 67.30%, and 68.08% of main-business revenue in 2023–2025 [^67]. The prospectus geography: mainland China 42.79% of FY2025 revenue, Hong Kong-booked 57.21%, genuinely overseas roughly 2.79% [^11].

The 2022 C+ round (¥8.39 billion raised) closed at a ¥107.8 billion post-money; March 2024 added ¥10.8 billion at just over ¥150 billion; and in June 2025 Alibaba Cloud paid ¥6.1 billion for 3.85% at 2.6302 yuan per share — a ¥158.4 billion mark, negotiated with full information rights, thirteen months ago [^60,90].

Fully half the offering is reserved for strategic investors: the sponsors' own investment arms (中金财富 and 中信建投投资) take 2.00% each under 24-month locks, 377 core employees subscribe up to RMB 1.593 billion under a 36-month lock, and as-yet-unnamed "large cooperating enterprises, large insurers, and national-level investment funds" absorb up to RMB 10.844 billion — the names surface only in the July 15 pricing announcement [^88].

The prospectus quantifies the exposure better than any short-seller could: at 2023–24 prices, CXMT's gross margin was −4.03% ex-writedown-reversals in 2024, and product-level gross margin once hit ==−108.76% on DDR4 and −121.62% on LPDDR4X== [^86].
Bernstein has put numbers on the collision: DRAM prices +43.2% in 2027 to $2.23/Gb, then {accent}−52.9% in 2028{/} to $1.05/Gb [^82].

In Seoul, the legal record is hardening: prosecutors indicted 10 people in December 2025 for leaking Samsung's 10nm-class DRAM process to CXMT, a former Samsung researcher drew a seven-year sentence in April 2026, and Korea's Supreme Court widened criminal exposure for tech-leak rings in February [^71,72,73].
ASML's China collapse from 41% of system sales to 19% is thus best read not as decoupling achieved but as the buying spree ending: CXMT now runs on its installed NXT-class base [^52].
CXMT and YMTC have "grown in capability and market share" — but the "overwhelming majority" of their output is sold within China.

As of July 2026, conventional DRAM contract growth has decelerated from ~90–95% QoQ (1Q26) to a forecast +13–18% (3Q26); DDR4 spot logged its first ~5% decline in about a year; consumer SSD spot fell 30–40%; phone OEMs are cutting memory content (flagships to 12GB) and unit targets (Transsion −40%, OPPO −20%, Vivo −15%); and IDC sees 2026 PC units down 11.3% — demand destruction, not new supply, is doing the rebalancing [^84].
